<details>
The script adds APT repository keys and sources during a post-installation step, which is a privileged operation that can lead to supply chain vulnerabilities. It copies GPG keys from the package's local `apt-keys/` directory into `/etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/` without verification, allowing an attacker who compromises the package build or distribution process to inject malicious keys. Worse, it automatically adds a third-party APT repository source (`https://repository.spotify.com`) to the system's package manager configuration if not already present.

APT repositories, especially when signed with trusted keys, have extremely high system privileges—they allow remote code execution as root via package updates. Automatically installing and trusting a proprietary repository without explicit user consent is a high-risk behavior. While Spotify is a legitimate vendor, this pattern weakens system security by expanding the attack surface to the availability and integrity of Spotify's repository infrastructure. The script also does not pin the repository or use a signed-by directive, increasing risk.

This behavior goes beyond standard packaging practices: legitimate AUR packages do not modify system package sources or trust stores. Such actions are reserved for the user or system administrator. The script's repository-adding logic, even if well-intentioned, constitutes an unauthorized escalation in system control and could be abused in a supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>
Adds unapproved APT repo and trusted keys automatically</summary>
</security_assessment>
